mnemonics.title: Graphics Pipeline Steps
mnemonics.content: MGRD: Modeling → Geometry → Rasterization → Display. Think 'My Geometry Reads Display'.

mnemonics.title: Input Devices
mnemonics.content: MJKLDT: Mouse, Joystick, Keyboard, Light Pen, Digitizer, Touch panel. 'Mouse Joins Keyboard Light Digit Touch'.

mnemonics.title: Output Devices
mnemonics.content: CPLP: CRT, Printer, LCD, Plotter. 'CRT Prints LCD Plots'.

mnemonics.title: RGB Colors
mnemonics.content: Red + Green + Blue = Light (Additive). Think: Light bulbs add up to white.

mnemonics.title: CMY Colors
mnemonics.content: Cyan + Magenta + Yellow = Ink (Subtractive). Think: Printing subtracts from white paper.

acronyms.usage: Traditional display technology using electron beams and phosphor screens
Flat-panel display technology using liquid crystals and backlight
Display technology using semiconductor light sources
Enhanced VGA with resolutions up to 800×600 or higher
Specialized memory for storing display data and pixel information
Printer resolution measurement indicating print density
Technology used in active matrix LCD displays

visualTips.visualization: Raster: Like painting with dots (paint-by-numbers); Vector: Like drawing with pen (connect the dots)
Think of a grid of colored squares (pixels) where each square holds a color value - like a digital canvas
Think of a flipbook - faster page flips create smoother animation; Hz is like 'flips per second'
More bits = more color choices. 1-bit = 2 colors (black/white), 8-bit = 256 colors, 24-bit = millions of colors
Two canvases - draw on one while showing the other, then swap. Like having two chalkboards.

analogies.analogy: Like a digital canvas where each pixel is a paint bucket storing a specific color, ready to be displayed on the screen frame.
Like the frame rate in movies - 24 frames/second looks smooth; similarly, higher Hz gives smoother display.
Like a single tile in a mosaic - individually small, but together form the complete picture.
Like the number of dots in a halftone print - more dots create finer detail and sharper images.
Like a factory assembly line - raw materials (3D data) go through multiple stages to become finished products (2D images).
